tagline: B2C pricing: a five-voice panel, a willingness-to-pay ladder, a €19 vs €49 head-to-head, and the decision they add up to.

project.title: Premium pricing study — Klar money coaching
project.goal: Find the monthly price for Klar's premium coaching tier: where willingness-to-pay breaks across segments, and whether a €19 Lite / €49 Pro split beats a single mid-price tier.
project.description: Klar is a subscription app for German private households: it connects accounts, explains pension gaps in plain language, and — in the premium tier — adds a quarterly 45-minute session with an independent, commission-free money coach. This study prices that premium tier with a five-voice panel spanning willingness-to-pay: a discovery council on what 'worth paying for' means, a €9–€79 price ladder, and a €19 Lite vs €49 Pro head-to-head, closed by a decision record. (Example project shipped with Sonaloop — reload or remove it any time: `sonaloop load-example premium-pricing-study` / `remove-example`.)

personas.description: Pediatric nurse, 34, in Lübeck; rotating shifts, €2,900 net, saves €150 a month into an ETF plan she half trusts; cancelled a Riester contract at a loss two years ago and has audited every subscription on her phone since.

identity_traits.appearance_notes: Practical short haircut, hospital lanyard tan line, usually in trainers because her feet hurt by Thursday.
identity_traits.avatar_profile: Female-read person, mid-thirties, pediatric nurse context, natural professional studio illustration, tired but warm eyes.
identity_traits.avatar_constraints: no logos, no readable name tag, no stereotype exaggeration

segment.attitude: burned once, twice shy — audits every euro that leaves her account on a schedule

demographics.household: single, rents a 2-room flat, one cat

role.title: Pediatric nurse (Kinderstation)
role.responsibilities: Three-shift rotation on a 28-bed children's ward; medication rounds, parent conversations, documentation backlog after every late shift.
role.seniority: 11 years on the ward, informal go-to for new colleagues
role.decision_power: full authority over her own money, none over her roster

company_context.stack: Sparkasse banking app, paper payslips, an Excel household book she updates on the 1st, Finanztip newsletter
company_context.operating_model: Money admin happens on the couch after late shift, twice a month, with tea and deep suspicion

goals: Get to €20,000 in savings without thinking about money daily
Understand whether her ETF plan is actually enough for retirement
Never again sign a financial product she can't explain to her sister
Reduce to 80% hours at 40 without losing the flat
constraints: €150/month is the realistic savings ceiling after rent and the car
Shift work kills any appointment that needs a weekday 10am slot
The Riester exit cost her €1,400 — every advisor now starts at minus 100 trust
Annual subscription audit: anything she can't justify in one sentence gets cancelled

relationships.name: Katrin
relationships.friction: keeps forwarding crypto videos Maren has asked her to stop sending

relationships.name: Herr Albers
relationships.friction: sold her the Riester contract; she still has to greet him at the branch

personality.working_style: methodical in bursts — ignores money for six weeks, then spends one Sunday putting everything in order
personality.communication_style: direct, slightly dry, asks 'what does that cost me in the worst case' before anything else
personality.risk_tolerance: low since the Riester loss; will accept boring if boring is explainable
personality.character_notes: Generous with people, ruthless with companies. Quotes exact euro amounts from memory.

pain_points: Can't tell whether financial content is advice or an affiliate funnel
The one free advisor session she trusts (Verbraucherzentrale) has an 11-week waiting list
Every app wants account access before it tells her anything useful
Pension statement arrives yearly and might as well be in Latin
success_criteria: She can explain her own setup in two sentences and mean it
No product in her life she wouldn't buy again at full price

personas.description: Software engineer, 29, in Munich; €5,400 net, optimizes everything from keyboard layouts to ETF TER basis points, reads r/Finanzen at lunch, distrusts anyone paid by commission, has a spreadsheet he is quietly proud of and never quite finishes.

identity_traits.appearance_notes: Merch hoodie from a conference, mechanical-keyboard posture, expensive backpack he researched for nine days.
identity_traits.avatar_profile: Male-read person, late twenties, software engineer context, natural professional studio illustration, alert and slightly impatient.
identity_traits.avatar_constraints: no logos, no readable name tag, no stereotype exaggeration

segment.attitude: wants validation from an expert, not education — he has done the reading

demographics.household: lives with partner, splits a too-expensive Maxvorstadt flat

role.title: Senior software engineer
role.responsibilities: Owns the payout service; on-call one week in six; mentors two juniors he worries about more than he admits.
role.decision_power: full over his money; shared budget talks with partner every quarter

company_context.stack: Scalable Capital, a 14-tab Google Sheet, r/Finanzen, Notion vault of finance bookmarks he never reopens
company_context.operating_model: Optimizes in sprints: two weekends a year of deep finance work, then nothing — the gap bothers him

goals: Hit €200k invested by 33 without thinking in basis points daily
Get one credentialed human to sanity-check the spreadsheet assumptions
Stop re-deriving the same Entgeltumwandlung decision every November
Buy a flat with his partner in 4–6 years without wrecking the equity plan
constraints: Time, not money: a €49 subscription is nothing, a 90-minute onboarding call is everything
Partner veto on any tool that needs both their account logins
Refuses commission-based advice categorically — fee-only or nothing
Gets bored of any app whose insights stop changing after month two

relationships.name: Lea
relationships.friction: finds his spreadsheet evangelism exhausting; wants one shared answer, not four scenarios

relationships.name: Deniz
relationships.friction: pays for a fee-only advisor and won't shut up about it, which annoys Jonas because it works

personality.working_style: deep-dive sprints, then total neglect; automates anything he has done twice
personality.communication_style: precise, benchmarks everything, says 'source?' in regular conversation
personality.risk_tolerance: high on paper, medium in practice — rebalances less bravely than he posts
personality.character_notes: The fear under the optimizing: that one wrong assumption in row 12 quietly costs him six figures over 30 years.

pain_points: Cannot verify whether his pension-gap math is right — no one qualified has ever looked at it
Every 'free' advisor he tried turned into a Rürup pitch within 20 minutes
Generic robo-advice is beneath the spreadsheet; real advice is opaque to price-compare
His Notion finance vault is a graveyard of good intentions
success_criteria: An expert he respects says 'your setup is correct' — or shows him exactly where it isn't
Quarterly money admin under one hour, including the conversation with Lea

personas.description: School secretary, 51, in Gelsenkirchen, divorced four years; part-time years in the 2000s left a pension gap she can feel in her stomach; keeps her money at the Sparkasse counter, distrusts apps that want account access, but lies awake doing retirement math she doesn't trust.

identity_traits.appearance_notes: Reading glasses pushed into her hair, cardigan over the chair, handwriting that wins prizes for legibility.
identity_traits.avatar_profile: Female-read person, early fifties, school office context, natural professional studio illustration, composed with a guarded smile.
identity_traits.avatar_constraints: no logos, no readable name tag, no stereotype exaggeration

segment.attitude: trusts people, not platforms — wants a voice and a name, fears being talked into things

role.title: School secretary
role.responsibilities: Runs the front office of a 950-student school: sick lines at 7:15, parent walk-ins, the school budget spreadsheet nobody else opens.
role.seniority: 23 years, the institutional memory of the building
role.decision_power: complete over her own finances since the divorce — which is exactly what frightens her

company_context.stack: Sparkasse counter and online banking on the family PC, paper folder system ('Ordner Rente'), pocket calculator, the kitchen table
company_context.operating_model: Money decisions get made slowly, in writing, after a phone call with her brother

goals: Know — in one number she believes — whether the pension plus the house is enough
Close at least part of the gap from the part-time years before 60
Never depend on her ex-husband's pension settlement being calculated correctly
Sleep through the night before the annual Renteninformation arrives
constraints: €2,250 net with a settled routine — new monthly costs are felt immediately
Will not connect her bank account to an app; the Sparkasse data stays at the Sparkasse
The divorce advisor billed €190/hour and made her feel stupid; never again
Comparison anchor: Mieterverein €90 a YEAR, ADAC €54 a year — that's what membership costs

relationships.name: Bernd
relationships.friction: means well, retired Beamter, his pension advice doesn't map to her Angestellte reality

relationships.name: Frau Yilmaz
relationships.friction: swears by a finance app Heike privately thinks is reckless

personality.working_style: thorough, paper-first, finishes what she starts or doesn't start it
personality.communication_style: polite, then suddenly very precise when she smells a sales script
personality.risk_tolerance: very low for products, moderate for people she has vetted
personality.character_notes: The divorce taught her she can handle hard things. It also taught her what professional fees do to a settlement.

pain_points: The Renteninformation number is abstract; her fear is specific
Every retirement calculator online ends in a lead-capture form
Banks address her as 'pre-retirement planning segment' and pitch products within minutes
She has nobody financially neutral: family is opinionated, advisors are paid to sell
success_criteria: A person with a name walked her through HER numbers and didn't sell her anything
A written one-page plan in the Ordner that her brother can't poke holes in

personas.description: Self-employed master electrician, 42, in Reutlingen; four employees, irregular income between €3,000 and €8,000 a month; private and business money hopelessly intertwined; pays his tax advisor €120 an hour and considers that the going rate for anyone who saves him real money.

role.title: Owner, Elektro Lanz GmbH
role.responsibilities: Quotes, scheduling, two apprentices, chasing unpaid invoices, and the actual electrical work nobody else can sign off.
role.decision_power: absolute and lonely — every financial decision in the company and household lands on him

goals: Untangle private retirement from business reserves — one clear line between the two
A pension answer that works with irregular income, not against it
Stop the Versorgungswerk vs. Rürup vs. ETF question from resurfacing every single year
Keep the option to sell the business at 58 and not need to

His tax advisor is excellent on taxes and explicitly refuses retirement-planning questions
Anything that smells like a bank pitch dies instantly — he's been a business customer for 12 years and knows the script

relationships.name: Sandra
relationships.type: wife, does the office two days a week
relationships.friction: wants the private buffer untouchable; he keeps borrowing from it for materials

relationships.name: Herr Maurer
relationships.friction: answers everything except the one question that keeps Tobias up: 'am I saving enough, in the right place?'

personality.working_style: decides fast once he trusts the source; until then, stalls for years
personality.communication_style: blunt, fair, allergic to jargon — 'sag's mir wie einem Kunden'
personality.risk_tolerance: high in business, oddly conservative in private savings
personality.character_notes: Measures every service against his own trade: show up, do it right, invoice transparently, stand behind it.

pain_points: The self-employed are an afterthought in every finance app — 'monthly income?' has no honest answer
Mixing of business and private accounts means he genuinely doesn't know his own savings rate
Retirement advice for Selbständige is either a Rürup ambush or a shrug
Sunday-morning research sessions end in more open tabs, never in a decision
success_criteria: One session that ends with a decision made, not 'options to consider'
A setup Sandra signs off on without the conversation becoming a fight

identity_traits.appearance_notes: Sharp casual style assembled secondhand, ring light visible in video calls, types faster on a phone than most people on a keyboard.

role.title: Social media manager

Stop the €75 ETF plan from being the first thing cancelled in a tight month
Understand taxes-when-freelancing before, not after, she takes the leap
A money setup she could explain on camera without embarrassment

relationships.name: Selin
relationships.friction: keeps suggesting a Bausparvertrag 'like we did it'; the conversation goes nowhere twice a year

relationships.name: Marc
relationships.friction: freelances full-time and makes it look easy, which makes her runway anxiety worse

personality.working_style: fast, mobile-first, batches life admin into sprints with a podcast on
personality.communication_style: warm, meme-fluent, asks uncomfortable questions cheerfully
personality.risk_tolerance: medium — will try anything for a month, commits to almost nothing

pain_points: Finance content is either boomer-coded or crypto-bro-coded; nothing speaks her dialect honestly
Every subscription she keeps is a small bet against her own freelance runway
Tax fear is the single biggest blocker to going freelance and no app addresses it head-on
She knows what things cost but not what they're worth — and she knows that about herself

The freelance-runway number goes up every month she keeps it

open_questions: Which segments clear €49/month, and what do they need to see before they do?
Is the human coaching session the thing people pay for, or the thing that makes them trust the app?
Where between €9 and €79 does monthly acceptance break?

hypotheses.text: At €19/month, at least 4 of the 5 panel voices rate the price acceptable (bargain or getting-expensive) — €19 is below everyone's pain line, including the budget-tight segment.

derived_from.question: Where between €9 and €79 does monthly acceptance break?

hypotheses.text: In a €19 Lite vs €49 Pro head-to-head, the panel splits by segment rather than converging: every self-employed and high-income-optimizer voice chooses Pro, every budget-tight voice chooses Lite.

prediction.metric: Pro share among self-employed + optimizer voters in the head-to-head

derived_from.question: Which segments clear €49/month, and what do they need to see before they do?

hypotheses.text: A one-tap pause (instead of cancel) halves voluntary churn among subscription-fatigued users in the first year — the quarterly cull spares products that offer a smaller commitment step.

prediction.metric: voluntary churn among subscription-fatigued users, with vs. without one-tap pause

councils.prompt: What would make money guidance worth paying for every month?
questions: What do you do today when a money decision scares you?
What would make you cancel a money app after three months?
councils.selection_reason: Five voices spanning the willingness-to-pay axis: two budget-tight employees, a high-income optimizer, a security-first planner, a self-employed owner — the segments the premium tier must price across.

statements.text: Honestly? I put it off. The Riester thing scared me into research and the research scared me into nothing. The one place I'd trust — Verbraucherzentrale — has an eleven-week waiting list. So the decision waits eleven weeks, or forever. If you want my €19, you're competing with 'forever', which is free.

Cancelling is my hobby, ask my sister. I cancel anything I can't justify in one sentence at my yearly audit. 'It told me my ETF plan is probably fine' — that's worth one sentence exactly once. What's the sentence in month four? If there isn't one, I'm gone, and I won't feel bad.

I build a spreadsheet. That's the problem — I already have the spreadsheet, fourteen tabs of it. What scares me isn't the decision, it's that nobody qualified has ever checked my assumptions. I don't need education, I need a code review for my retirement math. That's a different product than 'money tips' and I'd pay properly for it.

I cancel when the insights stop changing. Month one: 'your pension gap is €840/month', great, genuinely. Month three: same number, different notification. An app that tells me what I already know is a €0 product with a paid skin. The human session is the only part that can't go stale — if the coach is actually good.

I call my brother, and then I don't fully believe him, because Beamte live on a different planet. What I did for the divorce was pay €190 an hour to a man who made me feel stupid for asking. So today the scary decisions sit in a folder called 'Rente' and I do the math at night with a pocket calculator. I'm not proud of it. But I'm also not giving an app my bank login to fix it.

I wouldn't get to month three unless a person — a name, a voice, the same one each time — was part of it from the start. I'd cancel the moment it starts selling. I know the turn by heart now: first they explain, then they 'recommend', then there's a product. If your coach earns commission, this whole conversation never happened.

I ask Herr Maurer, my tax advisor, and he says 'that's not tax advice' — which is correct and useless. €120 an hour and the one question that matters is out of scope. So: yes, I'd pay for the missing person. But they have to handle 'my income is €3,000 in August and €8,000 in March' without putting me in a monthly-contribution box. Every app so far has choked on that sentence.

I cancel when nothing gets decided. I don't need a dashboard, I have Lexware, dashboards I've got. If the quarterly session ends with 'options to consider', that's a craftsman who walks the site, nods, and never sends the quote. One decision per session, written down, that Sandra can read — that's the whole job. Do that and the price is honestly secondary.

I watch three TikToks about it, cross-check the least insane one, and then mostly don't act. The tax-when-freelancing question has been 'scary, later' for two years — it's the actual wall between me and going freelance. I'd pay real money to have exactly that one fear taken apart by a human. But 'real money' for me is a number with a one in front.

Three months is exactly one cull cycle, so: it gets cancelled unless it had a 'moment' that quarter — something I'd screenshot. Also, professional note, since this is literally my job: let me pause instead of cancel. The cull is about feeling in control, not about the €19. Products with a pause button survive my cull at twice the rate. I know because I'm the one culling.

councils.exec_summary: Nobody on the panel wants 'money tips' — all five already have a coping mechanism (spreadsheet, brother, TikTok, tax advisor, procrastination) and each one has a specific hole the premium tier could fill: verification for Jonas, a trusted human for Heike, irregular-income competence for Tobias, one named fear (freelance taxes) for Aylin, and a one-sentence justification for Maren's yearly audit. Cancellation logic differs by segment: staleness kills it for the optimizer, sales-smell for the security-seeker, indecision for the self-employed, the quarterly cull for the subscription-fatigued. The human session is the only component every voice treats as non-stale — pricing should hang the premium on it.
councils.summary: Discovery council ahead of the pricing work. Q1 ('what do you do when a money decision scares you') surfaced the competing alternatives the price must beat: free-but-never (procrastination), free-but-slow (Verbraucherzentrale), trusted-but-wrong-domain (tax advisor, brother). Q2 ('what makes you cancel') produced segment-specific kill conditions and one concrete product demand — a pause option instead of cancel — flagged by the subscription-fatigued voice as churn-halving and recorded as an open hypothesis.

councils.prompt: Klar Premium — app plus a quarterly 45-minute session with an independent, commission-free money coach. React to every monthly price on the ladder.
councils.selection_reason: Same five voices as the discovery council — the ladder needs the full willingness-to-pay spread, including the budget holder of each household.

responses.quote: €9 I can justify in one sentence: 'cheaper than the gym I don't go to.' That survives my audit.
€19 is Netflix plus Spotify. It has to beat both every single month, and in a tight month, it won't.
€29 is a fifth of my monthly savings rate. The app would be eating the thing it's supposed to grow.
€49 a month is €588 a year. For that I can wait the eleven weeks for the Verbraucherzentrale, twice.
That's half my ETF rate. No sentence justifies that at my audit. Not even a paragraph.
At €9 the 'coach' is a chatbot with a calendar link. Real fee-only advice costs €150 an hour — the unit economics of €9 don't contain a human.
€19 is two office coffees a week. If the data layer alone kills my spreadsheet maintenance, fine — but at this price I assume the coach is the upsell.
€29 with a real quarterly session is under €90 per expert-hour-equivalent. Deniz pays €180 flat with his advisor. I'd take this and feel clever about it.
€49 needs the coach to actually engage with my spreadsheet, not read me a script about the Drei-Schichten-Modell. Show me the coach's credentials and I'm in; hide them and it's €49 for vibes.
€948 a year is real advisor territory — at that point I book Deniz's guy directly and own the relationship.
If a real person looks at my pension for €9 a month, then I'm not the customer, I'm the product. What are they selling about me to make that work?
€19 every month — that's €228 a year. My Mieterverein is €90 a year and they once saved me four hundred euros. Membership prices, I understand; this is more than double.
€29 monthly is the kind of cost that creeps. I'd rather pay once, properly, for one session with my numbers — a subscription assumes my pension gap changes every month. It doesn't.
For €49 a month I expect a person who knows my name in an office I can visit. An app cannot be that, at any price, and certainly not at this one.
That's almost what the divorce advisor cost, and I promised myself: never again a monthly bill I'm afraid of.
€9 is suspicious. In my trade, when a quote comes in at a third of the others, the wiring is aluminum. Who's the coach who works for €9-a-month customers?
€19 is one breakfast at the bakery with the crew. If it sorts the private-versus-business mess even halfway, it's the cheapest tool in my van.
€29 is nothing against the €1,400 a year Herr Maurer costs me — IF the session handles 'March €8,000, August €3,000' without a script error.
€49 I'd pay the way I price my own work: show up, decide something, put it in writing. The moment a session ends in 'options', I'm out — then it's an expensive dashboard.
At €79 monthly I'd expect them to also do the Lexware reconciliation. That's an employee, not an app.
€9 survives the cull on price alone — it's below my 'don't even think about it' line. The question is whether I'd respect it.
€19 is exactly my Adobe-after-discount trauma price. It's payable, but it's on the cull list every quarter and has to re-earn its spot with a screenshot moment.
€29 is more than my ETF plan in a rent month. An app that costs more than my investing is satire.
€49 monthly for me is freelance-client money. I'd sooner pay €150 once for one tax session than €49 forever.
€79 is my WG electricity share. The cull wouldn't even be a decision; it'd be a reflex.

statements.text: €9 actively damages the premise. The entire premium pitch is 'a real human verifies your numbers' — a €9 price tag says the human is decorative.
Cheap frightens me more than expensive. The divorce taught me to ask who pays the professional — if it isn't me, it's someone with an interest in my decisions.
€29 is the honest price for what's described. Below it I doubt the coach, above it I start comparing with Herr Maurer — and Herr Maurer has an office and liability insurance.
€19 is the edge of my world. Everything above it isn't 'expensive', it's simply not addressed to me — and I'd rather you knew that than pretended otherwise.

councils.exec_summary: The ladder breaks in one place: directly after €19. Every voice accepts €19 (acceptance 1.0) — then acceptance collapses to 2 of 5 at €29 and €49 (the optimizer and the self-employed owner), and to zero at €79. The cliff is not gradual, it is segmental: budget-tight voices exit entirely above €19, while the two high-WTP voices stay comfortable through €29 and only start demanding proof (credentials, decisions-in-writing) at €49. €9 backfires: it reads as 'suspiciously cheap' to exactly the three voices with the most money, who price the human coach at real-world rates and conclude a €9 human is fake. The data argues against any single price and for a tier split at the cliff edge.
councils.summary: Willingness-to-pay ladder for the premium tier, five rungs €9–€79, four-band van-Westendorp reactions grounded in each voice's stated budget anchors (savings rate, Mieterverein dues, tax-advisor hourly rate, subscription-cull threshold, WG electricity share). Acceptance: €9 → 0.4 (dragged down by too-cheap suspicion from the three highest-WTP voices, not by affordability), €19 → 1.0, €29 → 0.4, €49 → 0.4, €79 → 0.0. Only €19 clears majority acceptance; the cliff after €19 (−0.6) is the steepest possible on this panel. Segment view: budget-tight accepts only ≤€19; optimizer and self-employed accept €19–€49 with proof conditions; security-first accepts €19 only and rejects the subscription form itself above it.

councils.prompt: Klar Lite at €19/month vs Klar Pro at €49/month — which one would you actually subscribe to, and why?

options.label: A
options.title: Klar Lite — €19/month
options.text: Connected accounts with a plain-language pension-gap analysis, a weekly money digest, decision checklists, cancel or pause anytime. No human coaching.

options.label: B
options.title: Klar Pro — €49/month
options.text: Everything in Lite, plus a 45-minute video session every quarter with an independent, commission-free money coach (named, credentialed, the same person each time), a written action plan after every session, and priority chat between sessions.

councils.selection_reason: Same five-voice panel; the ladder said the panel splits at the cliff — the head-to-head tests whether the split is segmental and decisive enough to justify two tiers.

preferences.reason: €19 survives the yearly audit with the sentence 'it watches my pension so I don't have to.' €49 needs a paragraph, and paragraphs get cancelled.
The named, credentialed coach is the entire reason to pay. Lite is my spreadsheet with better fonts — Pro is the code review I can't get anywhere else for under €150/hour.

If I'm honest, B is the only one I actually want — a person, the same one each time. But €49 every month is a bill I'd be afraid of, and fear is what I'm trying to pay away. So A, reluctantly, as a trial of whether I can trust them at all.
Four sessions a year, each ending in a written decision, for less than five hours of Herr Maurer — that's the first retirement offer that fits how I actually buy services.
A gets a fair shot at surviving two culls. B is freelance-client money for a problem (taxes) I'd rather solve once, in one paid session, than rent forever.

statements.text: Lite at €19 sits exactly on my pain line — the ladder told you that and I'll repeat it: it's payable, not safe. It survives as long as the weekly digest keeps producing the one sentence I need for my audit.
Pro is for people whose problem is complexity. Mine is trust and €588 a year doesn't buy trust, it consumes it — that's a Riester contract's worth of money every two and a half years.
Lite is a nicely rendered version of work I've already done. The pension-gap number won't change month to month, so by week six Lite is a €19 screensaver.

Pro, if the coach's credentials are public and the session works on MY numbers, is underpriced — quarterly expert verification plus a written plan is what Deniz pays €720 a year for, without the data layer.
Lite I could try — €19 is over my Mieterverein anchor but not absurdly. What Lite cannot do is the one thing I need: be a person. A checklist doesn't notice when I'm being talked into something.
Pro describes exactly the person I've been looking for since the divorce — independent, named, no commission. If it were €49 once a quarter, I'd sign today. Monthly, it's a fear with a direct debit.
Lite assumes my problem is information. It isn't — it's decisions. Another dashboard joins Lexware in the pile of things that describe my mess without resolving it.

Pro is priced like a trade service: show up quarterly, decide something, leave paper. €588 a year against €1,400 for Herr Maurer — if the coach survives one session about irregular income, this is the cheapest professional in my life.
A is cull-survivable: pausable, cheap enough, and the digest could be my screenshot moment. It earns months one at a time, which is the only way anything earns months from me.

B is a commitment product sold to the commitment-averse. The coach is genuinely tempting for the tax fear — but quarterly cadence means I'd pay three months of €49 per useful hour. My math says: book one session somewhere, once.

councils.exec_summary: Lite wins on reach, 3:2 — but the margin is the least interesting number in the room. The split is perfectly segmental, exactly as the pre-registered bet predicted: both budget-tight voices and the security-first planner choose Lite; the optimizer and the self-employed owner choose Pro, both citing the same anchor (a real advisor's hourly rate) and the same condition (the coach must be named, credentialed, and decision-oriented). The most valuable single data point is Heike's reluctant A: she wants Pro's human and can't justify Pro's billing form — a quarterly billing experiment could convert her segment without touching the price. Conclusion: don't average this panel into one tier. Tier it.
councils.summary: Head-to-head of the two candidate tiers with full variant metadata (variant ids, per-persona presentation order randomized as the position-bias guard, and the pre-registered segment-split hypothesis). Result 3:2 for Lite, margin 0.2 (narrow), no abstentions. Segment splits: budget-tight 2–0 Lite, high-income optimizer 1–0 Pro, self-employed 1–0 Pro, security-first 1–0 Lite (reluctant, billing-form-driven). The Pro 'yes' votes both condition on coach credentials and written per-session decisions; the Lite votes condition on pause-ability and a recurring 'audit sentence'. Hypothesis h-pro-segments resolves validated: Pro share among self-employed + optimizer voters = 1.0.

hypothesis_results.note: All five voices accepted €19 (acceptance 1.0) — the bet under-called the floor, not the cliff. The informative part sits one rung higher: acceptance collapses to 2 of 5 at €29, and the two survivors are exactly the segments the Pro tier targets.

Clean segment split, no crossovers: optimizer and self-employed → Pro (2/2), budget-tight → Lite (2/2). The security-first voice chose Lite but for a billing-form reason, not a value reason — recorded in the head-to-head as the strongest argument for a quarterly-billing experiment on Pro.

syntheses.title: Pricing story: €19 buys reach, €49 buys trust — ship both tiers
syntheses.start_input: What can Klar charge for the premium coaching tier, and where does willingness-to-pay break?
syntheses.goal: Turn the discovery council, the price ladder and the tier head-to-head into one defensible pricing recommendation.

payload.arc_narrative: The arc ran in three moves. The discovery council established what the price would actually be buying: not information — every voice already has a coping mechanism — but a segment-specific missing piece: verification (Jonas), a trustworthy human (Heike), irregular-income competence (Tobias), one named fear dismantled (Aylin), a one-sentence justification for the yearly audit (Maren). The price ladder then located the structural break: unanimous acceptance at €19, a collapse to 2-of-5 at €29, zero at €79 — with the twist that €9 damaged credibility precisely among the highest-WTP voices, who priced the human coach at market rates and concluded a €9 human cannot exist. The head-to-head confirmed the break is segmental, not statistical: a 3:2 surface for Lite decomposing into a perfect split — budget-tight and security-first to Lite, optimizer and self-employed to Pro — validating the pre-registered bet (h-pro-segments) at 1.0. Reading the three runs together: the panel contains two different products' customers, and a single price would mis-serve both.
payload.gesamtbild: There is no single right price because there is no single buyer. Below €19 the product reads as suspicious to the people with money ('who pays the professional?' — Heike; 'the unit economics don't contain a human' — Jonas); above €19 it reads as unaffordable to the people without ('a fifth of my savings rate' — Maren; 'more than my ETF plan in a rent month' — Aylin). €19 is the reach price: the most any panel voice can renew without re-deliberating. €49 is the trust price: what the high-WTP segments will pay once the coach is named, credentialed, commission-free, and the session reliably ends in a written decision — their condition, stated twice, almost word for word. The dead zone between them (€29) is real: it buys neither reach nor enough coaching to satisfy the trust buyers. Two churn-side findings ride along: a pause option materially extends survival among the subscription-fatigued (Aylin, recorded as the open bet h-pause-churn), and quarterly billing — not a lower price — is what would convert the security-first segment to Pro (Heike's reluctant Lite vote).
payload.positionierung: Ship two tiers and let them be honestly different products: Lite at €19/month — the watchdog, sold on 'it watches your pension so you don't have to', pausable, cancel-proof copy, the audit sentence delivered weekly. Pro at €49/month — the professional, sold exclusively on the coach: name, face, credentials, fee-only badge, 'every session ends in a written decision'. Lead all acquisition with Lite (it is the only tier with panel-wide reach) and upsell Pro behind proof, not promises: after the first pension-gap number lands, show the coach who would walk through it. Do not launch a €29 middle tier — the ladder shows it sits in the dead zone. Test quarterly billing for Pro within two quarters; it is the single highest-leverage experiment this study surfaced.
payload.next_council_question: Does quarterly billing (€147/quarter) convert the security-first segment to Pro where monthly billing fails — and does it cannibalize annual-prepay willingness among optimizers?

references.role: What the price buys per segment: verification / human trust / irregular-income competence / one named fear / the audit sentence.
The structural break: acceptance 1.0 at €19 → 0.4 at €29 → 0.0 at €79; €9 backfires among high-WTP voices.
The split is segmental and pre-registered: budget-tight+security → Lite, optimizer+self-employed → Pro; margin 0.2 is reach, not preference strength.

citations.quote: €19 is one breakfast at the bakery with the crew. If it sorts the private-versus-business mess even halfway, it's the cheapest tool in my van.
If a real person looks at my pension for €9 a month, then I'm not the customer, I'm the product.
Pro describes exactly the person I've been looking for since the divorce — independent, named, no commission. If it were €49 once a quarter, I'd sign today. Monthly, it's a fear with a direct debit.

findings.text: Launch both tiers at the tested prices: Lite €19/month (pausable, reach product) and Pro €49/month (coach-led, trust product). Lead all marketing with Lite; gate the Pro upsell behind the first delivered pension-gap number plus the coach's named profile.

The €29 dead zone: a single mid-price tier is too expensive for the reach segments (ladder acceptance 0.4) and too thin to carry the coaching that justifies the trust segments' money — the seemingly safe compromise serves nobody on this panel.

High-WTP segments (optimizer, self-employed) buy the coach, not the app — both anchored on real advisor rates (€120–€180/h) and both conditioned on credentials and written per-session decisions. Their acceptance window is €19–€49 with proof.

Quarterly billing for Pro: the security-first segment wants Pro's human and refuses Pro's monthly billing form. €147/quarter may convert that segment at identical revenue — untested, highest-leverage follow-up.

statements.text: €19 is the edge of my world — payable, not safe. Everything above it isn't expensive, it's simply not addressed to me.
The named, credentialed coach is the entire reason to pay. Pro at €49 with public credentials is underpriced against the €150/hour alternative.
Pro is the person I've been looking for since the divorce. Monthly billing is the only thing standing between me and it.
Priced like a trade service: show up quarterly, decide something, leave paper. Cheapest professional in my life — if the session survives irregular income.
Lite earns months one at a time, which is the only way anything earns months from me. Give it a pause button and it survives the cull.

decisions.title: Ship two tiers — Lite at €19 and Pro at €49 — and lead all marketing with Lite
decisions.decision: Klar launches with exactly two premium prices: Lite at €19/month (full app, weekly digest, one-tap pause, no coaching) and Pro at €49/month (quarterly 45-minute session with a named, credentialed, fee-only coach plus a written action plan per session). No €29 middle tier. Acquisition leads with Lite as the reach product; the Pro upsell is shown only after the user's first pension-gap analysis, together with the coach's profile. Revisit triggers: (1) the quarterly-billing experiment for Pro (next study) changes security-first conversion, or (2) Lite's month-4 retention falls below the pause-adjusted target, which would indicate the audit-sentence value loop is not landing.

rejected.note: Single €29 tier rejected: the ladder places €29 in the dead zone — acceptance 0.4, too expensive for both budget-tight voices and the security-first planner, while too thin to fund the quarterly human coaching that is the only thing the two high-WTP segments said they would pay for.

Leading marketing with Pro rejected: Pro's appeal is conditional on proof (credentials, a delivered first insight) that a cold visitor hasn't seen yet, and the head-to-head shows its audience is 2 of 5 voices — Pro converts best as an upsell moment, not a landing page.

notes.title: Subscription fatigue is the real competitor
notes.text: Across all three runs, the price was never compared to value in the abstract — it was compared to the existing subscription stack and to each voice's personal cancellation ritual (Maren's yearly audit, Aylin's quarterly cull). The competitor isn't another finance app; it's the cull itself. Product implication confirmed twice: a one-tap pause converts a cancellation moment into a retention moment. Tracked as open hypothesis h-pause-churn.

notes.title: 'Independent' beats 'smart' in every trust moment
notes.text: Whenever a voice moved toward paying, the trigger word was an independence signal — 'commission-free', 'fee-only', 'named coach', 'the same person each time' — and never an intelligence signal. Nobody asked how clever the analysis is; three of five asked who pays the coach. Copy and pricing-page hierarchy should put the independence proof above the feature list, especially for the Pro tier.

sections.title: Willingness-to-pay evidence
sections.note: The pricing result chain: ladder cliff after €19, segmental tier split, and the synthesis that turns both into the two-tier recommendation.

sections.title: Messaging and trust
sections.note: What the copy must prove before either price is believable.
